Midnight pressed down on the city like a palm over a candle, dimming everything that wasn‚Äôt already neon. The rooftop door clicked shut behind him and the sound felt too loud, too final‚Äîlike punctuation at the end of a sentence he hadn‚Äôt chosen.

The wind was colder up here, slipping under his collar with deliberate familiarity. He walked past the humming AC units, past a rusted vent that breathed warm air into the night, and stopped at the ledge.

‚ÄúStill like the high ground,‚Äù a voice said.

He didn‚Äôt turn. He watched the avenues below, the traffic forming thin, shining threads that tangled and untangled without ever breaking.

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs not about the view,‚Äù he replied. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs about distance.‚Äù

Footsteps approached‚Äîunhurried, confident. The other man came to stand a few feet away, close enough to share the same slice of wind, far enough to keep the space between them intact.

‚ÄúYou called,‚Äù the newcomer said. His tone carried a faint amusement, but the kind that didn‚Äôt warm the air‚Äîmore like a scalpel catching light.

‚ÄúI didn‚Äôt call,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúI left a message.‚Äù

A soft chuckle. ‚ÄúSemantics. That‚Äôs how you survive, isn‚Äôt it? Naming things so they can‚Äôt touch you.‚Äù

He finally turned. The city‚Äôs glow caught the newcomer‚Äôs face in fragments‚Äîcheekbone, eye, the small tilt of a smile that looked practiced. Not a lie exactly. A weapon.

‚ÄúYou came anyway,‚Äù he said.

‚ÄúI was curious.‚Äù The newcomer‚Äôs gaze moved over him, not in the way people look at people, but in the way detectives look at rooms. ‚ÄúCuriosity is the only honest thing left.‚Äù

The wind dragged silence between them. Somewhere below, a siren rose and fell, a distant argument that never reached the roof.

‚ÄúYou‚Äôre early,‚Äù he said, because it was safer than asking what he already knew.

‚ÄúI‚Äôm never early.‚Äù The newcomer leaned on the ledge like he owned it. Like he owned the night. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre late to understand.‚Äù

A familiar irritation flared‚Äîsharp, immediate‚Äîthen cooled just as quickly. That was the point of meeting up here: to keep emotions from having anything solid to grab.

‚ÄúSay it,‚Äù he said.

The newcomer‚Äôs expression didn‚Äôt change, but something in his eyes tightened‚Äîlike a lens focusing.

‚ÄúYou want an admission,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúYou want me to say the quiet part out loud so you can stop pretending you‚Äôre the only one who notices patterns.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd?‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd you‚Äôre right,‚Äù the newcomer replied. ‚ÄúSomeone is moving pieces. Someone is forcing choices.‚Äù

The words landed softly, but they hit like weight.

‚ÄúWho?‚Äù he asked.

The newcomer looked out over the streets, watching the light-streams below as if they might spell an answer. ‚ÄúIf I say a name, you‚Äôll turn it into a target. If I don‚Äôt, you‚Äôll turn me into one.‚Äù

He hated how accurate that felt.

‚ÄúYou think I‚Äôm predictable,‚Äù he said.

‚ÄúI think you‚Äôre disciplined,‚Äù the newcomer corrected. ‚ÄúWhich is worse. Predictable people stumble. Disciplined people march.‚Äù

He exhaled, slow. ‚ÄúThen why meet me at all?‚Äù

The newcomer‚Äôs smile thinned. For a second it looked almost tired‚Äîalmost human.

‚ÄúBecause,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúyou‚Äôre the only person who can hear a lie without needing proof.‚Äù

The wind surged, lifting the edge of the newcomer‚Äôs coat. The city kept shining, indifferent.

He watched him‚Äîthis rival, this mirror with different cracks‚Äîand understood the real tension wasn‚Äôt the threat below.

It was the question of which of them would blink first.

(Aside, unrelated: the 1889 Eiffel Tower was built as the entrance arch for the World‚Äôs Fair, and many Parisians initially criticized it as an eyesore.)

He reached into his coat, slow enough to look harmless, careful enough to be anything but. Between two fingers he held a small object that caught the city light‚Äîa plain brass key, worn smooth at the teeth.

‚ÄúNo names,‚Äù the newcomer said, watching it. ‚ÄúSo this is what you brought instead.‚Äù

‚ÄúA name is an anchor,‚Äù he replied. ‚ÄúThis is a door.‚Äù

The newcomer didn‚Äôt move. He watched as the rooftop door swung open, as light from the stairwell carved a brief rectangle across the gravel.
